data.title: Ralph Lauren raises fiscal 2026 revenue growth outlook to low to mid-single digits amid strong global momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ralph Lauren Corporation (RL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Patrice Jean Louis Louvet opened the call by highlighting "a strong start to the fiscal year with first quarter results that exceeded our expectations across the top and bottom line." Louvet emphasized that "double-digit top line growth in both Asia and Europe, and high single-digit growth in North America" led to global comparable sales up 13%, surpassing prior guidance. He attributed the performance to "growing brand desirability, diversified drivers of growth and muscles of organizational agility and executional excellence."
  • Louvet cited the company's ability to expand its luxury lifestyle positioning, referencing the Shanghai Re-See event in China which delivered "a new record high for our China Q1 sales" and drove "over 64 billion impressions with strong customer acquisition." He added that the campaign contributed to adding "1.4 million new customers to our DTC businesses, a mid-single-digit increase to last year."
  • The CEO noted core product sales grew "mid-teens this quarter," with high-potential categories including women's apparel, outerwear, and handbags "increased strong double digits outpacing total company growth in the quarter."
  • Louvet highlighted the company's expansion into new markets, with "24 new owned and partner stores this quarter," and noted particularly strong performance in Asia, where "China grew more than 30% in the quarter, outpacing our competitive set."
  • Louvet described ongoing investments in technology and AI, stating "we will continue to expand this AI-driven program to additional core styles in fiscal '26" to drive sales and inventory efficiencies.
  • CFO Justin Picicci stated, "We delivered a strong start to fiscal '26 with continued progress on our long-term strategic priorities and first quarter results exceeding the expectations we laid out in May." He reported "total company first quarter revenue growth of 11% was above our high single-digit outlook," with Asia leading at "sales increasing 19% followed by Europe up 10% and North America up 8%."
  • Picicci added, "total company adjusted gross margin expanded 160 basis points to 72.1%... AUR increased 14% in the first quarter, exceeding our high single-digit outlook."

Outlook

  • Ralph Lauren raised its fiscal '26 constant currency revenues outlook "to increase low to mid-single digits, up from low single digits previously, led by growth in our Asia and Europe businesses." This represents a higher target than the previous quarter's "low single-digits" expectation.
  • The company expects "first half revenues now assumed up roughly high single to low double digits in constant currency."
  • For operating margin, management now projects "to expand approximately 40 basis points to 60 basis points in constant currency compared to our prior guidance of a modest increase."
  • Gross margins are expected "to be up slightly to last year, with AUR growth, discount reductions and favorable geographic and channel mix offsetting negative impacts from tariffs and noncotton material costs."
  • The company continues to express caution for the second half due to potential tariff-related pressures and broader consumer behavior.

Financial Results

  • Ralph Lauren reported "total company first quarter revenue growth of 11%" and "total company retail comps increased 13%, driven by an acceleration in our own digital business and ongoing momentum in our brick-and-mortar channels." Digital ecosystem sales grew "mid-teens."
  • Adjusted operating margin expanded 230 basis points to 16.6%, with operating profit increasing 29%.
  • Adjusted operating expenses declined 70 basis points to 55.5% of sales, with marketing investments increasing to 7.5% of sales.
  • The company ended the quarter with "$2.3 billion in cash and short-term investments and $1.6 billion in total debt." Net inventory increased 18% to last year, attributed to "strategic receipt pull forwards, including to mitigate the impact of tariffs in North America and foreign currency headwinds."
  • Ralph Lauren repurchased $250 million in shares in the first quarter.

Q&A

  • Matthew Robert Boss, JPMorgan: Asked about sustainability of business drivers and consumer outlook. Louvet responded that "these drivers are durable... strength of our brand desirability and value proposition, along with the continued high-quality recruiting of customers." Picicci explained the back half gross margin is challenged by tariffs, and "the big unknown... is the price sensitivity and how the consumer reacts to the broader pricing environment."
  • Jay Daniel Sole, UBS: Queried updated guidance and tariffs. Picicci noted the "updated guide reflects our overdelivery on Q1 as well as our latest view on Q2" and that "our latest expected tariff outlook specifically... is broadly in line with our May guidance."
  • Dana Lauren Telsey, Telsey Advisory Group: Asked about regional trends, AUR, and China acceleration. Picicci highlighted "strong comps across both our full-price stores and our outlet stores," and Louvet pointed to China now representing 9% of total company, "with major opportunities moving forward."
  • Paul David Kearney, Barclays: Inquired about inventory and tariff impact. Picicci clarified that inventory growth was "a little behind our double-digit top line growth for Q1 and right in line with our expected high single-digit top line growth for next quarter, Q2," adjusting for tariff-related strategic pull-ups.
  • John David Kernan, TD Cowen: Sought quantification of tariff impact on gross margin. Picicci said, "while tariff remains our biggest pressure point for the fiscal year, we're confident that we can more than offset that headwind and deliver year-over-year gross profit expansion."
  • Blake Anderson, Jefferies (on behalf of Ashley Helgans): Asked about SG&A leverage. Picicci explained, "we delivered SG&A leverage, and it really reflects better-than-expected sales and that drove some fixed expense leverage... Even as we drove that leverage in Q1, we invested behind our brand and our business."
  • Rakesh Babarbhai Patel, Raymond James: Asked about key city growth runway. Louvet responded, "we've called out top 30 cities, and we continue to see opportunities across these and very meaningful opportunities."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts adopted a positive tone, with frequent congratulations on strong results and probing questions about sustainability and growth drivers, but remained focused on risks tied to tariffs and macroeconomic headwinds.
  • Management's tone was confident in prepared remarks, using phrases like "we are encouraged" and "this strong early performance gives us confidence to take up our full year guidance," but shifted to a slightly cautious stance in Q&A regarding the second half outlook, emphasizing "proceed with caution" and "the big unknown... is the price sensitivity."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management sentiment remain positive, but there is heightened emphasis on macro risks and tariff uncertainty.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company increased its full-year constant currency revenue growth guidance from "low single digits" to "low to mid-single digits."
  • There is a maintained focus on brand elevation, digital expansion, and international growth, with a more explicit acknowledgment of tariff-related risks impacting the second half.
  • Key metrics such as revenue growth, gross margin, and operating margin showed improvement from the previous quarter.
  • Analysts continue to focus on margin sustainability, regional performance, and inventory management, but questions this quarter placed greater emphasis on tariff mitigation strategies and pricing power.
  • Management's confidence in their strategic pillars and international growth remains strong, though there is increased caution in forward-looking statements for the second half.

Risks and Concerns

  • Potential macroeconomic headwinds and tariff-related pressures in the U.S. are cited as key risks for the second half of the year.
  • Management is actively using a toolkit to "manage cost inflation headwinds," including supply chain diversification, cost efficiencies, and selective pricing actions.
  • Analysts raised concerns on how inventory, tariffs, and consumer sensitivity to pricing may affect margins and growth.

Final Takeaway

Ralph Lauren's first quarter of fiscal 2026 demonstrated robust global demand and successful execution of its brand elevation and growth strategies, leading to an upgraded revenue outlook. Management remains confident in the durability of its growth drivers and ability to offset tariff pressures, while continuing to invest in technology, new stores, and marketing to support long-term value creation. However, they remain cautious regarding the second half, closely monitoring consumer response to potential price increases and macroeconomic volatility.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Kimbell Royalty Partners affirms 2025 guidance as rig market share rises to 17% amid portfolio expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kimbell Royalty Partners, LP (KRP) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Robert Dean Ravnaas, Chairman and CEO, reported "solid results for the second quarter with strong cash flow, continued debt paydown and lower cash G&A cost per BOE." He highlighted that Kimbell's market share of U.S. land rigs actively drilling increased by 1% to 17%, noting, "while the overall U.S. land rig count dropped by 7% quarter-over-quarter as operators, primarily in the Permian, slowed drilling activity, our overall rig count dropped by only 2% to 88 rigs actively drilling on our acreage."
  • Ravnaas announced a $0.38 distribution per common unit and stated, "We remain encouraged by the opportunities we see in 2025 and beyond to continue to grow and expand our industry-leading portfolio of assets to generate long-term unitholder value."
  • President and CFO Robert Davis Ravnaas stated, "Oil, natural gas and NGL revenues totaled $75 million during the second quarter and run rate production was 25,355 BOE per day." He added, "second quarter general and administrative expenses were $9.6 million, $5.4 million of which was cash G&A expense or $2.36 per BOE. Total second quarter consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $63.8 million."
  • Davis Ravnaas further emphasized, "We continue to maintain a conservative balance sheet and remain very comfortable with our strong financial position, the support of our expanding bank syndicate and our financial flexibility."

Outlook

  • Management affirmed, "Today, we are also affirming our financial and operational guidance ranges for 2025. As a reminder, our full 2025 guidance outlook was included in the Q4 2024 earnings release."
  • The company expressed confidence in "continued robust development as we progress through 2025, given the number of rigs actively drilling on our acreage, especially in the Permian as well as our line of sight wells materially exceeding our maintenance well count."

Financial Results

  • Kimbell reported "Oil, natural gas and NGL revenues totaled $75 million during the second quarter and run rate production was 25,355 BOE per day."
  • "Second quarter general and administrative expenses were $9.6 million, $5.4 million of which was cash G&A expense or $2.36 per BOE."
  • "Total second quarter consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $63.8 million."
  • The company announced a cash distribution of $0.38 per common unit for the second quarter, with approximately 100% of this distribution expected to be considered a return of capital.
  • As of June 30, 2025, debt outstanding under the secured revolving credit facility was approximately $462 million, with $163 million in undrawn capacity.

Q&A

  • Timothy A. Rezvan, KeyBanc Capital Markets: Asked about the board’s stance on operator partnerships amid industry consolidation and how the company is prioritizing such opportunities. Robert Davis Ravnaas responded that partnerships are "an option that we've explored in the past. We continue to explore that," but emphasized organic growth and M&A for deals under $500 million as a primary value lever. He added, "We continue to maintain just a business-as-usual approach to making acquisitions, but certainly an operating partnership, if it makes sense for us, would be a wonderful thing."
  • Rezvan followed up on Kimbell’s Permian focus and whether opportunity sets are shifting. Davis Ravnaas explained, "We're always going to look at opportunities based on what makes the most sense across every basin in the United States...We have made, I would say, an outsized Permian acquisition move in the last few years. That's really just been where the opportunity set was."
  • John Annis, Texas Capital Bank: Asked about the resilience of rig activity on Kimbell’s acreage. Davis Ravnaas attributed it to their "asset base that is of higher quality than average and its diversified nature across the Permian Basin."
  • Annis also inquired about natural gas production trends. Davis Ravnaas said, "We would expect a slightly gassier mix in our production profile going forward. But nothing quite yet to where I would say it's really noteworthy or substantial."
  • Noah B. Hungness, Bank of America: Questioned changes in the M&A market and if cash flow multiples are adjusting. Davis Ravnaas responded, "Usually, these kinds of things take time. Either sellers don't transact or they get enough feedback...they need to expect a lower price and then they start to run up against exit constraints and timing."
  • Hungness asked about the stickiness of G&A costs. Matthew S. Daly answered, "It was mainly due to lower professional fees this quarter...For the rest of the year, you should target for modeling purposes sort of the lower end of guidance."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on partnership priorities, M&A rationales, Permian focus, and cost discipline, reflecting a neutral to slightly cautious tone as they sought clarification on strategy and sustainability of performance.
  • Management maintained a consistently confident tone, repeatedly affirming guidance and growth prospects. CEO Ravnaas stated, "We remain encouraged by the opportunities we see in 2025 and beyond."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts sustained their focus on M&A, leverage, and activity levels, but questions reflected increased attention to portfolio shifts and market risk.
  • Management’s confidence remained steady, with language such as "We continue to maintain a conservative balance sheet and remain very comfortable with our strong financial position."

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company’s rig market share increased from 16% in Q1 to 17% in Q2, with the total rig count on Kimbell’s acreage declining from 90 to 88, while overall U.S. land rigs fell by 7%.
  • Oil, natural gas, and NGL revenues decreased from $90 million in Q1 to $75 million in Q2, while consolidated adjusted EBITDA dropped from $75.5 million to $63.8 million.
  • Cash distribution per common unit decreased from $0.47 in Q1 to $0.38 in Q2, but the return of capital portion increased from 70% to approximately 100%.
  • Debt outstanding rose from $299 million to $462 million, while undrawn capacity decreased from $251 million to $163 million, reflecting balance sheet adjustments following facility expansion and preferred unit redemption.
  • Both quarters saw management reaffirming 2025 guidance and highlighting robust development activity and a diversified asset base.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that overall U.S. land rig count declined by 7% quarter-over-quarter, with the Mid-Con region experiencing a decline in drilling activity.
  • The company acknowledged that Permian acquisition opportunities have slowed, with Davis Ravnaas stating, "The Permian is probably at a lower share of activity from a sell-side perspective today than it has been over the last couple of years."
  • Davis Ravnaas cautioned that M&A valuations may need to adjust downward as growth expectations moderate, indicating, "Cash flow multiples on acquisitions are going to have to come down if you don't see that same growth profile in the Permian."
  • G&A cost discipline was cited as a focus area, but management signaled the current quarter’s low G&A was mainly due to lower professional fees, and future quarters may revert to the lower end of guidance.

Final Takeaway

Kimbell Royalty Partners delivered a strong operational quarter, maintaining high rig market share and affirming their 2025 guidance despite a broader industry slowdown and reduced commodity revenues. Management emphasized portfolio resilience, continued focus on disciplined M&A, and a conservative financial posture, positioning the company for future value creation amid evolving market conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AIG signals $500M annual cost savings and projects strong property profitability amid strategic reinsurance moves
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American International Group, Inc. (AIG) (AIG) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Peter Salvatore Zaffino reported "an outstanding second quarter," highlighting that adjusted after-tax income per diluted share reached $1.81, a 56% increase year-over-year, and adjusted after-tax income totaled $1 billion, up 35%. Zaffino noted that "our general insurance business... had underwriting income of $626 million, an increase of 46% year-over-year." He emphasized progress on strategic, operational, and financial objectives and stated, "We returned $2 billion of capital to shareholders, bringing the year-to-date total to $4.5 billion." Zaffino also announced the appointment of John Neal as President, overseeing General Insurance, effective December 1, describing him as "one of the most accomplished executives in our industry."
  • Zaffino highlighted the completion of AIG Next, which delivered "$500 million in savings and significant operational improvements," and mentioned "both S&P Global and Moody's upgraded their financial strength ratings of AIG's insurance subsidiaries during the quarter."
  • CFO Keith Francis Walsh stated, "Overall, total adjusted pretax income, or APTI, was $1.4 billion, an increase of 37% from the prior year quarter. This was driven by excellent results from the business and focused execution of our investment portfolio strategy."

Outlook

  • Zaffino reiterated, "We remain on track to achieve our 10% plus core operating ROE target in 2025 and continue to make steady progress on the long-term financial targets we outlined at our Investor Day."
  • Walsh indicated, "We remain on track to reduce our expense ratio below 30% by 2027."
  • No explicit guidance comparison to analysts' estimates was provided due to lack of sufficient data.

Financial Results

  • AIG reported net premiums written of $6.9 billion, an increase of 1% year-over-year, with 3% growth in Global Commercial and a 4% increase in North America Commercial Insurance net premiums written. Retail Casualty and Lexington Casualty each increased 19%, and Western World rose 15%.
  • Net investment income on an adjusted pretax basis was $955 million, up 9% year-over-year. General Insurance net investment income was $871 million, growing 17% year-over-year. Walsh reported, "General Insurance gross premiums written were $10.1 billion in the second quarter, an increase of 4% from the prior year."
  • The General Insurance accident year combined ratio as adjusted was 88.4%. Calendar year combined ratio was 89.3%, an improvement of 320 basis points from prior year quarter.
  • Capital return included $2 billion to shareholders in Q2 and $4.5 billion year-to-date. The quarterly dividend increased by 12.5% to $0.45 per share.
  • Book value per share at June 30 was $74.14, up 8% from June 30, 2024. Adjusted tangible book value per share was $69.81, up 4% from a year earlier.

Q&A

  • Taylor Alexander Scott, Barclays, asked about property pricing implications and reinsurance impacts. CEO Zaffino responded that "we are a big buyer of reinsurance on property... we benefit because if the rates are going down on reinsurance, on CAT as an example, that does benefit the original pricing." He added, "the combined ratio could go up a bit... if it goes into the low 80s, it's still a great business."
  • Scott followed up on capital deployment if growth slows. Zaffino said, "if we can't deploy the capital for growth, we will return it to shareholders... but I don't think that's the place we are."
  • Donald John Bailey commented, "Rates are very strong in the casualty market right now... both Lex Casualty and Retail Casualty grew rather substantially 19% in the second quarter."
  • Jon Hancock emphasized Specialty growth: "We've delivered 5% growth in the quarter, 7% year-to-date. For sure, there is increasing competition and rate pressure... but global specialty... we have real clear differentiated proposition."
  • Meyer Shields, KBW, inquired about reserve reapportionment and social inflation impacts. Zaffino explained, "we prudently reserved for our expected net loss exposure and the outcome... have been in line with our expected net loss estimates."
  • Elyse Beth Greenspan, Wells Fargo, queried about pricing trends. Zaffino replied, "We're not going to break it down by line... I think we are covering loss cost on casualty and other lines, excluding property."
  • Michael David Zaremski, BMO, questioned expense ratio improvements and E&S market dynamics. Zaffino commented, "the first half was a little bit more bumpy than I think the back half will be," and noted, "there's no evidence from us that it's slowing down in terms of submission count."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, with questions focused on pricing, capital deployment, and growth sustainability. There was some probing on reserve adequacy and expense management, but no overt skepticism.
  • Management displayed confident and measured language in prepared remarks and Q&A, with Zaffino using phrases like "we are well positioned," and "we remain encouraged." There was occasional caution regarding future property market conditions.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained similarly constructive, while management's confidence appeared more pronounced following the upgrades and operational achievements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a shift in management focus toward operational execution, cost savings, and capital returns following the completion of AIG Next. There was increased emphasis on property portfolio profitability and strategic use of reinsurance.
  • Management tone in Q2 reflected heightened confidence, supported by ratings upgrades and substantial cost reductions. In Q1, the tone was optimistic but more focused on growth and digital strategy.
  • Analysts' questions in both quarters centered on pricing, expense ratios, and capital management. The Q2 session placed more emphasis on reserve and reinsurance strategies.
  • Key metrics such as adjusted after-tax income per share, underwriting income, and book value per share improved compared to Q1. Guidance language in Q2 was more assertive regarding expense ratio targets and capital returns.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management discussed industry-wide uncertainty in property markets, noting potential volatility from catastrophe frequency and pricing pressures. Zaffino described the company’s property underwriting approach as "conservative."
  • Russia aviation-related claims were addressed, with Zaffino stating, "we prudently reserved for our expected net loss exposure and the outcome... have been in line with our expected net loss estimates."
  • Expense management remains a focus, as lean parent implementation and shifting of costs to business segments may create short-term fluctuations in reported ratios.

Final Takeaway

AIG management emphasized the company’s improved profitability, capital strength, and operational discipline, highlighted by a 56% year-over-year increase in adjusted after-tax income per share and $500 million in annual run rate savings from AIG Next. Strategic portfolio and reinsurance actions are expected to sustain strong property profitability, while continued investment in technology and a new leadership appointment are poised to support further growth and stability. Management’s guidance remains centered on achieving a core operating ROE of over 10% in 2025 and reducing the expense ratio below 30% by 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Aon exploring sale of Wealthspire Advisors - report
data.description:
• Aon (NYSE:AON) is evaluating the sale of Wealthspire Advisors, a $31 billion wealth manager it acquired when it purchased insurance brokerage NFP. 
  • AOL has hired UBS to serve as a financial adviser for the sales process, according to a Citywire report, which cited sources familiar with the matter.

data.title: Eli Lilly raises 2025 revenue guidance to $60B-$62B while expanding GLP-1 pipeline and production
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO David A. Ricks highlighted a strong quarter, stating, "Q2 was a strong quarter. We delivered robust revenue growth, shared top line clinical data from multiple Phase III programs and invested in several initiatives that will support our future growth." Ricks announced positive top-line data from the ATTAIN-1 orforglipron trial, with patients on the highest dose losing more than 27 pounds, or 12.4% of their body weight. He emphasized, "Our goal from the beginning was to create a medicine that has a clinical profile consistent with approved GLP-1 while offering the convenience of a once-daily pill and the production flexibility of small molecule chemistry to meet global demand."
  • Ricks also described recent milestones: "We achieved several key milestones since our last earnings call. These include the U.S. FDA approval of a new dosing schedule for Kisunla, the positive European CHMP opinion for Kisunla, and announced positive results in the SURPASS-CVOT Phase III trial for tirzepatide in people with type 2 diabetes and heart disease."
  • The company completed the acquisitions of SiteOne Therapeutics and Verve Therapeutics, adding to the pain and cardiovascular disease portfolios respectively.
  • Ricks addressed drug pricing reform, noting, “At Lilly, we've already implemented several initiatives, which directly lower patient costs for our most commonly used medicines.”
  • CFO Lucas E. Montarce stated, "Revenue grew 38% compared to Q2 2024, driven by our key products. Gross margin as a percentage of revenue was 85% in Q2, an increase of 3 percentage points versus the same quarter last year." Montarce further reported, "Earnings per share increased 61% to $6.31, including of a negative impact of $0.14 from acquired IPR&D charges."

Outlook

  • Montarce announced, “We are increasing the bottom and the top end of the revenue range as well as our expectation for performance margins and earnings per share. We now anticipate our revenue will be between $60 billion and $62 billion.” Non-GAAP earnings per share guidance was raised to $21.75 to $23, with performance margin expected between 43% and 45.5% of revenue.
  • The company expects to produce at least 1.8x the number of salable incretin doses in the second half of 2025 compared to the same period in 2024.
  • Management indicated the 2025 impact of currently announced tariffs will be modest and factored into guidance.

Financial Results

  • Revenue increased 38% year-over-year, with growth attributed to products including Ebglyss, Jaypirca, Kisunla, Mounjaro, Omvoh, Verzenio, and Zepbound.
  • Gross margin reached 85%, up 3 percentage points from the prior year, driven by improved production costs and product mix, partially offset by lower realized prices.
  • Non-GAAP performance margin was 45.9%. The effective tax rate was 16.5%.
  • U.S. revenue growth was driven by Zepbound and Mounjaro, though partially offset by an 8% price decline. European revenue grew 77% in constant currency, while Japan and China also saw strong Mounjaro-driven growth.
  • Mounjaro posted $5.2 billion in global sales and became the U.S. market leader in total type 2 diabetes incretin prescriptions. Zepbound contributed $3.4 billion in sales and continues to lead the U.S. branded anti-obesity market.

Q&A

  • Christopher Thomas Schott, JPMorgan: Asked about orforglipron’s weight loss profile versus competitors. Kenneth L. Custer responded that orforglipron offers “27 pounds of weight loss from a single pill and also get really encouraging effects on other important biomarkers... Now you're getting that all from a single oral pill, but we can manufacture at scale.”
  • Seamus Christopher Fernandez, Guggenheim: Probed on pricing environment and compounding. Ricks stated, "We've always had a philosophy across all medicines, including with incretins, to price to value."
  • Geoffrey Christopher Meacham, Citibank: Sought detail on GI adverse event rates for orforglipron. Daniel M. Skovronsky explained, “The GI profile was as expected for a GLP-1 agonist... most of the side effects occur early... and then they go down over time.”
  • Timothy Minton Anderson, BofA Securities: Inquired about Canadian generics' impact. Ilya Yuffa said, “We're seeing that our offering with the vial with Zepbound and the profile... is meeting a need for patients even in the context of noise, whether you have compounded or sema in the market.”
  • David Reed Risinger, Leerink Partners: Asked about U.S. employer coverage for anti-obesity medicines. Yuffa reported employer opt-in coverage has been steady around 50% to 55% and expects new benefit designs to increase opt-in over time.
  • Terence C. Flynn, Morgan Stanley: Questioned expectations for upcoming orforglipron trials. Custer stated, “We expect similarly encouraging results” and are preparing for regulatory submission by year-end.
  • Additional analyst questions covered topics including side effect profiles, gender split in trials, LillyDirect’s evolution, channel dynamics, discontinuation rates, pricing parity between the U.S. and Europe, and the cash pay channel strategy.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was probing and, at times, skeptical regarding orforglipron’s competitive efficacy and pricing dynamics, while seeking clarity on Zepbound’s market response to PBM decisions and channel evolution.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone throughout, emphasizing strong results, robust pipeline progress, and readiness to address policy and market access challenges. Quotes such as "We are very pleased with what we disclosed this morning" and "We remain confident in the long-term growth outlook for Zepbound" reflect this confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts’ skepticism about orforglipron’s weight loss profile and competitive positioning appeared to increase. Management’s tone on pricing and market access was more defensive in response to persistent questions about PBM dynamics and price erosion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was raised this quarter, with revenue and EPS projections increased, while in Q1 guidance was reaffirmed.
  • The company reported continued market share gains for Mounjaro and Zepbound, with Mounjaro reaching U.S. market leadership, up from strong Q1 growth.
  • Manufacturing capacity expansion accelerated, with explicit figures for production increases and new facility plans in Q2.
  • Q2 featured more discussion of PBM formulary headwinds, especially regarding the CVS decision’s impact on Zepbound, whereas Q1 acknowledged such risks but described the impact as limited.
  • Analyst focus shifted from broad product launches and pipeline milestones in Q1 to more pointed questions about competitive dynamics, PBM access, and orforglipron’s real-world positioning in Q2.
  • Management confidence in orforglipron’s potential was consistent, though the Q2 call saw more direct challenges to its efficacy data versus competitors.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing price declines, with U.S. revenue growth partially offset by an 8% price decline and expectations for continued single-digit net price erosion.
  • The exclusion of Zepbound from CVS’s template formulary was identified as a significant headwind for prescription growth in Q3.
  • Regulatory and policy risks around drug pricing reform remain, with management warning that imposing foreign price controls on the U.S. market "risks embracing the worst of 2 worlds" and could harm innovation.
  • Analyst concerns included the impact of potential Canadian generics, discontinuation rates in orforglipron trials, and the sustainability of employer opt-in rates for anti-obesity coverage.

Final Takeaway

Eli Lilly management emphasized another quarter of robust revenue growth and clinical milestones, highlighted by strong performances from Mounjaro and Zepbound, the advancement of orforglipron as an oral GLP-1, and an increase in 2025 revenue and earnings guidance. The company is scaling manufacturing capacity and navigating pricing and access challenges while maintaining confidence in the long-term growth trajectory of its key products and pipeline. Management reiterated a commitment to patient access, innovation, and disciplined execution as the company works to sustain momentum into the second half of 2025.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Kenvue outlines low single-digit organic sales decline for 2025 as leadership transitions and strategic review progress
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kenvue (KVUE) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Kirk L. Perry, Interim CEO, addressed his first earnings call, emphasizing the urgency of leadership changes and a comprehensive strategic alternatives review, stating the Board is “making changes in leadership, working Kenvue on an accelerated growth trajectory in progressing our comprehensive review of strategic alternatives.” Perry brings extensive experience from Procter & Gamble and Google, aiming to drive a turnaround by focusing on execution, simplification, and a consumer-centric approach. He stressed, “The Kenvue Board has taken a set of decisive actions to enable the company to unlock shareholder value and reach its full potential.”
  • Perry highlighted operational complexity, noting, “We can't be everything to everyone, and we need to be more choiceful on where to play and how to win.” He announced new leadership appointments, including Andy Dasgupta as Asia Pacific lead and Mike Wondrasch as Chief Technology and Data Officer, stating, “With these moves, we've appointed 4 new leaders to Kenvue's leadership team over the past 3 months which is 1/3 of the team.”
  • Amit Banati, CFO, shared, “Enhancing operating rigor and discipline will be a key priority for me. We have a significant opportunity to improve our overall integrated business planning process with a particular focus on consumption-driven demand forecasting.” Banati cited a need for cost discipline and improved productivity across the supply chain and SG&A.

Outlook

  • The company revised its 2025 guidance, now expecting organic sales to be down low single digits with about neutral impact from currency. Banati stated, “For 2025, we currently expect organic sales to be down low single digits with about neutral impact from currency.”
  • Adjusted operating margin is anticipated to contract versus last year, largely due to strategic price investments, fixed cost deleverage, and inflationary pressures. “We are assuming adjusted operating margin will contract versus last year... given the timing of tariffs,” Banati said.
  • Full year adjusted diluted EPS is now expected in the range of $1 to $1.05, including a low single-digit drag from currency.
  • Compared to Q1, this represents a shift from the prior guidance of organic sales growth in the 2% to 4% range and flat EPS.

Financial Results

  • Kenvue reported a Q2 organic sales decline of 4.2%. Banati explained, “Organic sales in quarter 2 declined 4.2%. While global consumption was positive and significantly outpaced organic sales performance across each segment, this was a disappointing result in what remains a dynamic macro backdrop.”
  • Adjusted operating margin contracted 10 basis points year-over-year to 22.7% and adjusted gross margin contracted 70 basis points to 60.9%.
  • Adjusted diluted EPS was $0.29 versus $0.32 in the prior year period. Adjusted net income was $560 million.
  • Segment performance: Self Care organic sales contracted 5.9%, Skin Health and Beauty declined 3.7%, and Essential Health decreased 2.4%. Perry noted that “Self Care remains a bright spot when it comes to the strength of our brands and the underlying health of the business,” despite overall top line softness.

Q&A

  • Lauren Rae Lieberman, Barclays: Asked about focusing on core brands and markets for immediate impact versus longer-term complexity reduction. Perry responded, “What you said is absolutely true. We need to focus... the biggest brands, the biggest countries, the biggest initiatives to help us grow more quickly.”
  • Peter K. Grom, UBS: Queried the biggest surprises and turnaround opportunities. Perry cited “self-induced complexity” as an inhibitor but highlighted focus areas such as Tylenol and regional strength in EMEA and Latin America.
  • Javier T. Escalante Manzo, Evercore ISI: Sought clarity on strategic review and possible divestitures. Perry reiterated, “We're not going to obviously comment on any individual business segment... but I also know and believe that execution has played a big role in our performance to date.”
  • Andrea Faria Teixeira, JPMorgan: Asked about measuring success in the turnaround, particularly for Skin Health and Beauty. Perry said, “The ultimate success is going to be determined by consumers voting for us every day at the shelf. We need to be holding ourselves accountable for seeing sequential improvement in that vote.”
  • Stephen Robert R. Powers, Deutsche Bank: Probed headwinds to growth from complexity reduction and restructuring costs. Banati replied, “It's a little premature to comment on the specifics... the Board is undertaking a strategic review that's looking at all potential alternatives.”
  • Filippo Falorni, Citi: Asked about sales trends and margin contraction. Banati stated, “So far in July, we're seeing similar trends to quarter 2. So consumption is ahead of organic sales. I think the seasons are similar to what we saw.”
  • Bonnie Lee Herzog, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about Q3 trends and impact from seasonal shifts. Banati confirmed, “So far in July, we're seeing similar trends to quarter 2. So consumption is ahead of organic sales. I think the seasons are similar to what we saw.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly negative, largely focused on operational complexity, turnaround timing, and execution risks. Questions reflected a desire for clarity on strategy and measurable outcomes.
  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was confident but acknowledged shortcomings, with Perry stating, “Our results are not where we want them to be, and we're going to focus on our 4 operating priorities to drive improved performance.” During Q&A, management was measured, acknowledging challenges and avoiding specifics on restructuring or divestitures.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from cautiously optimistic to more urgent and action-oriented, with greater emphasis on decisive interventions and accountability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The prior quarter outlined organic sales growth of 2% to 4% and flat adjusted diluted EPS, whereas Q2 revised guidance to low single-digit declines for organic sales and $1 to $1.05 in adjusted diluted EPS.
  • The strategic focus has shifted from brand investment and operational optimization to urgent complexity reduction, portfolio review, and immediate leadership changes.
  • Analysts in Q2 were more focused on execution risks, complexity, and turnaround metrics, compared to Q1 where questions centered on innovation and category dynamics.
  • Management’s confidence in turnaround was more tempered in Q2, with explicit recognition of underperformance and a call for urgent improvement.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted significant operational complexity, inventory fluctuations, and category deceleration as major challenges.
  • Tariff impacts remain fluid, with a possible annualized impact of $150 million.
  • Margin contraction is expected due to price investments, fixed cost deleverage, and inflationary pressures.
  • Analysts remain concerned about the pace of turnaround, restructuring costs, and the impact of strategic alternatives review on long-term growth.

Final Takeaway

Kenvue’s Q2 2025 earnings call marked a pivotal moment, with leadership transitions, a comprehensive strategic review, and a decisive shift in operational focus. The company revised its outlook to a low single-digit decline in organic sales for the year, with margin pressures persisting. Management outlined immediate priorities around simplification, execution, and portfolio optimization, while signaling further updates as the strategic review progresses. The tone was candid about current underperformance and resolute in the commitment to improve execution and unlock shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sunrun surges after posting surprise Q2 profit with record 70% storage attachment rate
data.description:
Sunrun (NASDAQ:RUN) +28.7% in Thursday's trading after reporting a surprise Q2 profit as revenues jumped 8.7% Y/Y to $569M, driven by a quarterly record 70% storage attachment rate and cost efficiencies.

Sunrun (NASDAQ:RUN) said it installed a record high 392 MWh of storage capacity during Q2, 48% more than the year-earlier quarter, and 227 MW of solar capacity, up 18% Y/Y, while subscriber additions increased 15% to 28,823 and the company had 941,701 subscribers as of June 30.

Q2 aggregate subscriber value jumped 40% Y/Y to $1.6B, and contracted net value creation reached a record high $376M, more than quadruple the year-ago quarter.

For the full year, Sunrun (NASDAQ:RUN) sees aggregate subscriber value at $5.7B-$6B, representing 14% growth compared to FY 2024 at the midpoint; contracted net value creation of $1B-$1.3B, up from prior guidance of $650M-$850M and representing 67% Y/Y growth at the midpoint; and cash generation of $200M-$500M, unchanged from the company's previous guidance.

Sunrun (RUN) is well positioned to capitalize on a shift toward third-party ownership offerings, where it has about a third of the market share, Susquehanna analysts Charles Minervino and Eric Clay wrote in maintaining their Positive rating on the stock and raising their price target to $13 from $12.

While industry-wide installations are expected to fall in 2026 on the elimination of the residential clean energy tax credits at the end of this year, Sunrun (RUN) hopes to grow volumes as the market shifts focus, the Susquehanna analysts said.

data.title: Americold stock retreats after 2025 guidance cut on demand headwinds
data.description:
Americold Realty Trust (NYSE:COLD) shares gapped down 8.5% in Thursday afternoon trading after the REIT lowered the midpoint of its full-year guidance for adjusted funds from operations, amid a challenging environment for cold storage demand. Still, its second-quarter earnings came in better than expected.

The operator of temperature-controlled warehouses now expects adjusted FFO per share of $1.39-$1.45 (midpoint $1.42 vs. $1.10 average analyst estimate, compared with $1.42-$1.52 (midpoint $1.47) in the prior view.

“Looking to the second half of the year, we expect the challenging demand environment to continue, with occupancy and throughput levels remaining below typical seasonality trends," said CEO George Chappelle.

Meanwhile, Q2 adjusted FFO of $0.36, matching the consensus, surpassing the $0.27 consensus, edged up from $0.34 in the prior quarter and slid from $0.38 a year earlier.

Revenue of $650.7M, vs. $643.7M expected, rose from $629.0M in Q1 and dipped from $661.0M in Q2 2024.

Core EBITDA was $159.1M, up from $147.6M in the earlier quarter and down from $165.5M in the year-ago period.

"Cold storage demand continues to be impacted by macro headwinds that have moderated 2H25 occupancy assumptions, which tracks with the continued weakness in the USDA inventory data, Citi analyst Craig Mailman wrote in a note.

Physical occupancy fell to 62.8% from 63.3% in Q1 and 67.8% in 2024's Q2.

Mailman added that "external growth remains a driver, but top line organic growth could remain tepid in the near term."

data.title: Long-term mortgage rates hit lowest level since April
data.description:
This week, long-term mortgage rates fell to its lowest level since April, according to the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.63% as of August 7, down from 6.72% last week but higher than 6.47% in the same period a year ago.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.75%, compared to 5.85% in the prior week and 5.63% a year ago.

"The decline in rates increases prospective homebuyers' purchasing power and our research shows that buyers can save thousands by getting quotes from a few different lenders," said Freddie Mac's chief economist, Sam Khater.
